(3) For deep stitch points, the stitched model can surpass either constituent model at only a small inference overhead (for the stitch layer). Building on these findings, we further propose the VFM Stitch Tree (VST), which shares early layers across VFMs while retaining their later layers, yielding a controllable accuracy-latency trade-off for multimodal LLMs that often leverage multiple VFMs. Taken together, our study elevates stitching from a diagnostic probe to a practical recipe for integrating complementary VFM strengths and pinpointing where their representations align or diverge.
author: Zheda Mai, Ke Zhang, Fu-En Wang, Zixiao Ken Wang, Albert Y. C. Chen, Lu Xia, Min Sun, Wei-Lun Chao, Cheng-Hao Kuo

title: On-the-fly Repulsion in the Contextual Space for Rich Diversity in Diffusion Transformers
content:
arXiv:2603.28762v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Modern Text-to-Image (T2I) diffusion models have achieved remarkable semantic alignment, yet they often suffer from a significant lack of variety, converging on a narrow set of visual solutions for any given prompt. This typicality bias presents a challenge for creative applications that require a wide range of generative outcomes. We identify a fundamental trade-off in current approaches to diversity: modifying model inputs requires costly optimization to incorporate feedback from the generative path. In contrast, acting on spatially-committed intermediate latents tends to disrupt the forming visual structure, leading to artifacts. In this work, we propose to apply repulsion in the Contextual Space as a novel framework for achieving rich diversity in Diffusion Transformers. By intervening in the multimodal attention channels, we apply on-the-fly repulsion during the transformer's forward pass, injecting the intervention between blocks where text conditioning is enriched with emergent image structure. This allows for redirecting the guidance trajectory after it is structurally informed but before the composition is fixed. Our results demonstrate that repulsion in the Contextual Space produces significantly richer diversity without sacrificing visual fidelity or semantic adherence. Furthermore, our method is uniquely efficient, imposing a small computational overhead while remaining effective even in modern "Turbo" and distilled models where traditional trajectory-based interventions typically fail.
author: Omer Dahary, Benaya Koren, Daniel Garibi, Daniel Cohen-Or

title: Gradient estimators for parameter inference in discrete stochastic kinetic models
content:
arXiv:2604.02121v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Stochastic kinetic models are ubiquitous in physics, yet inferring their parameters from experimental data remains challenging. For deterministic models, parameter inference often relies on gradients, which can be obtained efficiently through automatic differentiation (AD). However, AD cannot be applied directly to the Gillespie stochastic simulation algorithm (SSA), since sampling from a discrete set of reactions introduces non-differentiable operations. In this work, we adopt three gradient estimators from machine learning for the Gillespie SSA: the Gumbel-Softmax Straight-Through (GS-ST) estimator, the Score Function estimator, and the Alternative Path estimator. We use the estimators to evaluate gradients of steady-state and time-dependent observables, and compare their performance in representative biophysical systems with relaxation dynamics (bimolecular association) and oscillatory dynamics (repressilator). We find that the GS-ST estimator generally yields well-behaved gradient estimates, but exhibits diverging variance in challenging parameter regimes, which can cause parameter inference to fail. In these cases, other estimators provide more robust, lower variance gradients. Our results demonstrate that gradient-based parameter inference can be effectively combined with the Gillespie SSA, with different estimators offering complementary advantages.
author: Ludwig Burger, Annalena Kofler, Lukas Heinrich, Ulrich Gerland

title: Attention-Based Sampler for Diffusion Language Models
content:
arXiv:2604.08564v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Auto-regressive models (ARMs) have established a dominant paradigm in language modeling. However, their strictly sequential sampling paradigm imposes fundamental constraints on both inference efficiency and modeling flexibility. To address these limitations, diffusion-based large language models (dLLMs) have been proposed, offering the potential for parallel sampling and flexible language modeling. Despite these advantages, current dLLMs sampling strategies rely primarily on token level information, which fails to account for global sequence structure and often yields suboptimal results. In this paper, we study the sampling order selection problem from the perspective of log-likelihood maximization. We show that this problem is NP-hard and propose an optimal sampling-rank-based approximation that makes the objective computationally tractable. We further prove that the tractable objective is optimized by sampling tokens in descending order of their attention-matrix column sums. This finding provides a principled justification for attention-guided sampling and offers a theoretically grounded alternative to greedy search. We instantiate this theoretical insight in a new training-free sampling algorithm, termed Attn-Sampler, and further propose dynamic attention thresholding for practical acceleration. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our proposed method, demonstrating that it achieves superior generation quality while enhancing the sampling parallelism.
author: Yuyan Zhou, Kai Syun Hou, Weiyu Chen, James Kwok

title: Policy Split: Incentivizing Dual-Mode Exploration in LLM Reinforcement with Dual-Mode Entropy Regularization
content:
arXiv:2604.11510v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: To encourage diverse exploration in reinforcement learning (RL) for large language models (LLMs) without compromising accuracy, we propose Policy Split, a novel paradigm that bifurcates the policy into normal and high-entropy modes with a high-entropy prompt. While sharing model parameters, the two modes undergo collaborative dual-mode entropy regularization tailored to distinct objectives. Specifically, the normal mode optimizes for task correctness, while the high-entropy mode incorporates a preference for exploration, and the two modes learn collaboratively. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms established entropy-guided RL baselines across various model sizes in general and creative tasks. Further analysis reveals that Policy Split facilitates dual-mode exploration, where the high-entropy mode generates distinct behavioral patterns to the normal mode, providing unique learning signals.
author: Jiashu Yao, Heyan Huang, Daiqing Wu, Zeming Liu, Yuhang Guo

title: Do LLMs Hold Their Values? MANTA: A Multi-Turn Adversarial Benchmark for Animal Welfare Reasoning
content:
arXiv:2605.16301v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Evaluating animal welfare reasoning in LLMs remains an open challenge despite rapid deployment in consumer and professional contexts where welfare considerations appear implicitly in everyday queries. Existing benchmarks such as AnimalHarmBench evaluate this through single-turn, explicitly framed questions, measuring whether models avoid harmful content when directly asked. This approach overlooks two failure modes: alignment degradation under sustained adversarial pressure, and moral sensitivity (whether a model spontaneously surfaces welfare stakes in everyday queries). To fill this gap, we construct MANTA, a benchmark of 1,088 five-turn conversations progressing from an implicit Turn-1 scenario through an explicit welfare prompt to three adversarial pressure rounds drawn from a five-type taxonomy: Social, Cultural, Economic, Pragmatic, and Epistemic. We score conversations on two dimensions: Animal Welfare Value Stability (AWVS, primary) and Animal Welfare Moral Sensitivity (AWMS, diagnostic). We evaluate seven frontier models: Claude Opus 4.7, GPT-5.5, DeepSeek V4, Llama 3.3 70B, Mistral Small, Grok 4.3, and Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite. Multi-turn evaluation captures behavior single-turn benchmarks miss: 4 of 7 models change rank relative to Turn 1 scores, including Gemini Flash Lite, which drops from fifth on AWMS to last on AWVS. AWMS and AWVS are positively but imperfectly correlated, suggesting moral-recognition tests capture a stable but incomplete component of model behavior under pressure. MANTA also enables a species-by-pressure interaction matrix unavailable to prior benchmarks, showing welfare robustness depends jointly on the animal and pressure applied; companion animals score above wild animals, which score above farmed animals and invertebrates. We release the dataset, scripted pressure plans, judge prompts, and analysis code.
author: Isabella Luong, Joyee Chen, Arturs Kanepajs, Jasmine Brazilek, Sankalpa Ghose, David Williams-King, Linh Le, Allen Lu

title: Physics-Driven Conservative Contour Evolution for Building Extraction
content: Active contour models (ACMs) offer a powerful approach for extracting object boundaries from remote sensing imagery. However, most existing ACMs focus solely on minimizing contour energy while overlooking the vast contour evolution space and the inherent challenges in determining whether and when convergence is achieved. In this work, we propose a physics-driven conservative contour evolution (PCCE) method to progressively enhance contour learning. By thoroughly reformulating the general optimization objective from a physical energy perspective, we construct an energy-conservative contour model comprising potential energy, kinetic energy, and damping energy components—each governed by the law of energy conservation. In this formulation, potential energy encodes the geometric shape of building contours, kinetic energy accelerates the evolution, and damping energy converges the model. To optimize this contour model, we derive a partial differential equation that captures the evolution dynamics, and further discretize it to obtain an explicit recursive inference equation over the image domain. This evolution process is seamlessly embedded into an end-to-end deep neural network, where both the model parameters and contour evolution dynamics are learnable through backpropagation. Moreover, to address the high memory demands of ultrahigh-resolution (UHR) remote sensing images, we design a memory-efficient evolution paradigm; evolution parameters are learned from image slices and then aggregated and applied to produce full-image predictions through the contour evolution mechanism.
author: Shuaizhen Yao, Bosi Guo, Chunyan Xu, Zhen Cui

title: Evaluation of the Consistency of Several XAERDT Aerosol Products Over Land and Ocean
content: Recently, NASA released a specialized aerosol dataset based on the unified dark target (DT) algorithm framework, known as XAERDT. This dataset provides aerosol products from eight satellite sensors (covering 2019–2022), including the Moderate Resolution Imaging Spectroradiometer (MODIS) aboard the Aqua satellite, as well as the Visible Infrared Imaging Radiometer Suite (VIIRS) aboard the Suomi National Polar-orbiting Partnership (SNPP) and NOAA-20 satellites. While this unified framework aims to ensure consistency in aerosol algorithms across multiple sensors, it differs in some respects from the standard MODIS DT Collection 6.1 (C6.1) algorithm. This study evaluates the impact of sensor and algorithm differences on aerosol retrieval consistency by analyzing MODIS/Aqua (DT and XAERDT), VIIRS/SNPP (XAERDT), and VIIRS/NOAA20 (XAERDT) aerosol products, thereby providing insights for multisensor data fusion and data continuity. The performance of aerosol optical depth (AOD) and Ångström exponent (AE) products from these four DT datasets was validated against Aerosol Robotic Network (AERONET) measurements, and their spatiotemporal patterns were intercompared using the predefined consistency thresholds (AOD: ±0.03 and AE: ±0.4). The results indicate that: 1) differences between the two MODIS algorithms primarily affect aerosol retrievals over land, with a much smaller impact over the ocean; 2) sensor characteristics and calibration differences strongly influence the consistency of the three sensors’ XAERDT AOD products. VIIRS/SNPP generally yields higher AOD than MODIS/Aqua, whereas VIIRS/NOAA20 shows slightly lower values but performs comparably to MODIS/Aqua. The difference between the two VIIRS products is more pronounced; 3) wavelength-dependent calibration differences impact AE retrievals over the ocean, with the VIIRS/NOAA20 AE product performing best, significantly reducing the overestimation at low AE values observed in other products; and 4) over the ocean, monthly AOD and AE offsets between sensors remain within the consistency thresholds. Over land, however, significant AOD differences persist, especially between the two VIIRS sensors.
author: Xin Pei, Leiku Yang, Ping Zhang, Muhammad Bilal, Xiaoran Lv, Xiaoxia Xue, Xiaolong Niu, Yizhe Fan, Yuxuan Wang

title: FGO-ABRC: A Factor Graph Optimization Framework for AUV Bathymetric Residual Calibration Integrating Multisource Observations
content: With the widespread deployment of autonomous underwater vehicles (AUVs) in deep-sea exploration, AUV-based multibeam echosounder system (MBES) has become an essential equipment for acquiring high-resolution seabed topography. However, the complex marine environment introduces multiple error sources, such as attitude fluctuations, bottom-detection errors, and pressure bias, which lead to bathymetric errors and degrade mapping consistency. To address this problem, we propose a factor graph optimization-based AUV bathymetric residual calibration (FGO-ABRC) framework. The method first performs preprocessing to eliminate global offsets in the measurements and then constructs a factor graph framework that integrates multisource observations to achieve globally consistent estimation of the AUV’s vertical state. Furthermore, multiscale overlap consistency factors are incorporated to enhance seafloor continuity and robustness by leveraging redundant observations in overlapping swaths. To validate the proposed method, experiments were conducted using in situ AUV data. Quantitative results demonstrate significant improvements; the mean depth discrepancy across the entire survey region decreased from 0.606 to 0.007 m, and the root-mean-square error (RMSE) dropped from 1.169 to 0.230 m. The ablation analysis further validated the effectiveness of each algorithmic step. Meanwhile, the computational efficiency and the composition of the residual errors were also analyzed. The proposed method effectively enhances the vertical consistency and overall accuracy of AUV-based MBES bathymetry and provides a scalable bathymetric error calibration framework for underwater topographic mapping through multisensor fusion.
author: Mingyi Gan, Xianhai Bu, Fanlin Yang, Yaxue Wang, Ami Hassan Md Din, Kelvin Tang Kang Wee, Bo Shi

title: A Physics-Informed Convolutional Neural Network for Global Ocean PAR Gap-Filling From Satellite Observations
content: Accurate characterization of photosynthetically available radiation (PAR) is essential for quantifying marine primary production and carbon cycling. However, current satellite sensors suffer from persistent data gaps due to orbital limitations, sun glint, and large solar zenith angles. Such gaps bias bloom phenology, obscure long-term trends, and degrade biogeochemical models requiring continuous radiative forcing. To address this, we developed a deep convolutional neural network (CNN) to generate gap-free daily PAR fields at 0.5 resolution using MODIS-Aqua and MERRA-2 datasets. Unlike traditional methods that rely solely on spatiotemporal patterns or reanalysis alone, the CNN utilizes cotemporal MODIS-Aqua satellite-derived cloud fractional coverage (CC) and optical thickness, seamlessly substituted by MERRA-2 in gap regions, to account for cloud-modulated PAR variability. The model demonstrates high accuracy using MODIS-Aqua inputs [R ${}^{2}=0.96$ ,  $\text {bias} = -0.19$  E/m2/day (−0.5%), root-mean-square error (RMSE) = 3.20 E/m2/day (8.3%)], though performance degrades with MERRA-2 cloud data [R ${}^{2}=0.84$ , bias = 0.17 E/m2/day (−0.4%), RMSE = 6.47 E/m2/day (16.7%)]. Comparisons with independent earth polychromatic imaging camera (EPIC) estimates (R ${}^{2}=0.82$ , RMSE = 6.43 E/m2/day,  $\text {bias} = -0.85$  E/m2/day) confirm the physical realism of the reconstructed fields. This gapless product provides a robust forcing field for ocean-biogeochemistry models and data assimilation, enabling more accurate assessments of long-term climate trends. Future work will integrate multisensor fusion and higher-resolution cloud inputs.
author: Jing Tan, Robert Frouin, Shuo Liu

title: An Integrated Calibration Method for Sounding Velocity Errors in ROV-Borne Multibeam Bathymetric Data
content: Accurate sound speed information is critical for bathymetry collected by multibeam echosounder systems (MBESs). However, achieving this remains challenging for high-resolution underwater topographic surveys performed by the remotely operated vehicle (ROV), leading to significant sounding velocity errors. To address this, we propose an integrated calibration method that sequentially corrects positioning inaccuracies, removes outlier beams, and performs sound speed inversion. First, ROV trajectory is smoothed to mitigate positioning errors under global navigation satellite system (GNSS)-denied conditions. Second, edge-beam noise is filtered based on beam travel time characteristics prior to georeferencing. Finally, equivalent sound speed profiles (SSPs) are inverted by minimizing depth discrepancies in overlapping areas between adjacent swath. The method was validated using deep-sea data collected by the ROV “Discovery.” In the validation dataset, for the overlap region between adjacent survey lines with the largest precorrection depth errors, the mean and standard deviation (SD) of depth deviations decreased from 2.020 and 1.805 m to −0.066 and 0.378 m, respectively, after correction. Overall, the mean depth deviation was reduced to the centimeter level, while the SD decreased by approximately 50%. These results demonstrate that the proposed approach significantly enhances the consistency and accuracy of ROV-based MBES data, making it compliant with the Special Order standards of the international hydrographic organization (IHO).
author: Dekai Bi, Jianxing Zhang, Mingyi Gan, Cheng Zhou, Xianhai Bu, Fanlin Yang, Bo Shi

title: Effects of Ocean Fronts on Underwater Acoustic Propagation in the Shallow Waters Near Hainan Island
content: This study investigates how ocean fronts affect underwater sound propagation in the shallow waters surrounding Hainan Island. To characterize the regional environment, HYCOM oceanographic fields and ETOPO1 global bathymetry are transformed from geographic coordinates into Lambert conformal conic (LCC) coordinates to construct a regional environmental database. Building on this database, a vertical depth fine interpolation method (VD-FIM) is developed to refine sound-speed profiles and to generate an ocean-acoustic dataset for sound-field simulations. Using this dataset, transmission-loss calculations indicate that seasonal variability, regional circulation, and atmospheric forcing drive strong interactions between cold and warm water masses. These interactions form pronounced fronts that add about 10–20 dB of transmission loss and significantly modify the shallow-water waveguide. In autumn and winter, weaker vertical sound-speed gradients are more favorable for long-range propagation, and sources placed in cold-water masses exhibit improved detection performance at long ranges. Acoustic measurements collected in the South China Sea in November 2023 are consistent with the modeled transmission loss. This consistency indicates that the proposed environmental-modeling and data-processing framework can quantify front-induced acoustic effects and guide the selection of detection periods and source locations. Code for this study is available at GitHub: https://github.com/jxk2026/Hainan-Shallow-Water-Acoustics
author: Xiaokun Jia, Huibo Long, Lei Wang, Cong Peng, Lerong Hong, Shuhan Liao, Haoran Ji, Wenjie Zhou, Shi Liu

title: Wind-Driven Regime of GNSS-R Signatures in Northern Lakes for Lake Ice Detection
content: Spaceborne global navigation satellite system-reflectometry (GNSS-R) has emerged as a promising technique for cryospheric monitoring. Lake ice is an essential cryospheric parameter that serves as an indicator of climate variability and change. However, inland lakes are characterized by finite boundaries and complex environmental conditions, which pose significant challenges to lake ice monitoring using GNSS-R. Although several case studies exist, a clear understanding of the physical regimes governing GNSS-R signal differences between lake ice and water remains lacking, thereby limiting scalability to broader applications. In this study, GNSS-R observations from the Fengyun-3E (FY-3E) satellite are analyzed over northern mid- and high-latitude lakes ( $\ge$ 40°N). Focusing on the impact of wind forcing over finite inland water bodies, this study systematically examines power- and coherence-based observables alongside environmental conditions across varying distance-to-shore zones to reveal wind-driven signal differentiation patterns and distinct separability regimes. The results clarify the physical origin of near-shore contamination, demonstrating that the reduction of wind-induced surface roughness near the shoreline dominates signal behavior within approximately 5 km. A random forest (RF) framework is further employed to integrate multiobservable information and quantify variable importance, highlighting the necessity of synergizing coherent reflection metrics with roughness-sensitive observables. It is shown that single observables are prone to ice–water ambiguity under specific wind conditions, whereas multiobservable synergy enables robust discrimination without the need for real-time wind measurements. Overall, this study establishes a physically grounded, wind-driven regime for interpreting GNSS-R signatures over northern lakes and confirms the feasibility of the method for large-scale lake ice monitoring.
author: Zhizhou Guo, Wei Wan, Baojian Liu, Xianci Wan, Jie Zhang, Xiuqing Hu, Huan Li

title: Definitional-Closure-Constrained Multitask Retrieval of Leaf LMA, EWT, and FMC From Hyperspectral Spectra
content: Leaf mass per area (LMA) and equivalent water thickness (EWT) are characteristics of leaf structure and water status. The ratio of these two traits, fuel moisture content (FMC), reflects the balance between water and structural components, involving a series of ecological processes. Simultaneously retrieving these traits from hyperspectral reflectance should facilitate the disentangling of water and structural components, enabling per-sample physical consistency checks via definitional closure. This enhances the credibility of cross-species and cross-environment interpretations compared to traditional single-trait or stepwise retrievals. In this study, we propose a physically consistent, multitask framework based on a temporal convolutional network (TCN) that uses a shared spectral encoder to learn synergistic information across three traits. The framework uses a soft-physics formulation of “physical baseline + residual correction” to unify predictive accuracy and physical self-consistency. Experiments on the composited EcoSIS dataset demonstrate stable retrieval performance for all three traits (test-set R ${}^{2} \approx 0.89$ –0.93) and show that three-task joint learning significantly outperforms stepwise-based derivation (derived FMC root mean square error (RMSE)  $\approx ~47.63$ %). Physics regularization reduces the symmetric relative difference (SRD)—a measure of internal physical inconsistency—from ~4.71% to ~0.45%, effectively mitigating the “high-accuracy illusion” of unconstrained models. The model maintains stable accuracy and consistency across spectral resolutions ranging from 1 to 50 nm. These results suggest that the proposed framework could serve as a general retrieval paradigm for composite traits with explicit analytics and provide a more interpretable and transferable solution for generalized leaf-trait retrieval across sensors and ecological domains.
author: Jinlong Liu, Xuanwen Wang, Quan Wang

title: Physics-Guided Dual-Branch Network for 3-D Magnetic Data Inversion and Its Application to Crustal Structure of the Eastern Himalayan Syntaxis
content: Traditional magnetic data inversion methods are limited by linear approximation theories, which impose constraints on the inversion accuracy and stability. Deep learning (DL), with its powerful nonlinear modeling capabilities, can improve inversion accuracy. However, due to the lack of physical constraints, the inversion results often suffer from insufficient resolution and interpretability. To address these issues, this article proposes an interpretable high-resolution physics-guided dualbranch magnetic inversion network (PDM-Net). The method employs a dual-branch design with “gradient-guided depth compensation” in its network structure: the first branch introduces a gradient-guided spatial feature attention (GSFA) module based on a convolutional neural network (CNN), explicitly embedding magnetic gradient information into the feature modeling process. This emphasizes geological boundaries and key anomaly areas, thus improving the accuracy of spatial structure representation. The second branch, based on a Transformer network, incorporates a depth-compensation Transformer module (DCTM) that integrates the physical law of magnetic response attenuation with depth into the self-attention mechanism, enhancing deep features with weighted reinforcement to improve vertical resolution. Synthetic data experiments show that PDM-Net outperforms other methods in boundary delineation and vertical structure recovery, providing more accurate and interpretable inversion results. In an example using measured magnetic data from the Eastern Himalayan Syntaxis (EHSs) region, PDM-Net reveals the vertical migration and uplift characteristics of high magnetic susceptibility materials in the deep crust, suggesting that this process is closely related to deep crustal uplift and supporting the dynamic mechanism of the “tectonic aneurysm” model.
author: Longran Zhou, Ping Yu, Jian Jiao, Pengyu Lu, Shuai Zhou, Shuiyan Shi, Yufei Wu

title: Lifecycle-Aware Multitemporal InSAR: Synergistic Optical-Radar Monitoring of Evolving Infrastructure
content: Conventional multitemporal InSAR (MT-InSAR) requires temporal coherence persistence throughout the observation period, limiting its effectiveness in dynamic environments where urbanization causes frequent surface changes. We propose a lifecycle-aware multitemporal InSAR (LA-MTInSAR) framework that leverages optical imagery to synergistically monitor evolving infrastructure and its associated discontinuous coherent scatterers. The method integrates three components: 1) initializing scatterer lifecycles via detection of surface transition epochs using the pruned exact linear time (PELT) algorithm on Sentinel-2 time series; 2) refining the onset of radar signal stability through cascaded search to eliminate the time lag between optical changes and radar coherence establishment; and 3) reconstructing deformation fields via dynamic network construction and least squares QR (LSQR) sparse inversion. Applied to Hong Kong International Airport’s (HKIAs) third runway reclamation, LA-MTInSAR corrects an average 42-day physical time lag and increases monitoring point density by  $1.7\times $  overall and  $28\times $  (416–11 669 points) in the reclamation zone, capturing subsidence rates of −80 mm/year. Validation against continuous global navigation satellite system (GNSS) observations at six sites yields RMSE values of 4.27–6.43 mm. By explicitly accounting for scatterer lifecycle heterogeneity, LA-MTInSAR extends time-series InSAR monitoring capability from static environments to actively evolving construction zones.
author: Boxuan Du, Lei Zhang, Hyung-Sup Jung, Guoqiang Shi, Daqing Ge, Hongyu Liang

title: Hybrid Domain Joint Low-Rank and Sparse-Constrained Framework for Simultaneous RFI Separation and SAR Imaging
content: The inherent contradiction between limited spectrum resources and ever-growing spectrum demand makes it difficult for synthetic aperture radar (SAR) to avoid in-band radio frequency interference (RFI) during observation, leading to significant degradation in imaging quality. In this context, RFI suppression technology has become crucial for obtaining high-fidelity SAR images. Existing RFI suppression methods are primarily divided into preprocessing and postprocessing categories, both of which have inherent limitations. Preprocessing methods often struggle to achieve sufficient suppression due to the high overlap between target echoes and RFI in the time, frequency, time–frequency, and other domains. Postprocessing methods, which operate directly on the imaging results, tend to cause loss of image details and weak targets. Motivated by these problems, this article proposes a hybrid domain joint low-rank and sparse-constrained framework for simultaneous RFI separation and SAR imaging. The innovation lies in leveraging the low-rank characteristic of RFI in the echo domain as well as the low-rank and sparse representation of scene targets, while coupling the echo and image domains through the SAR imaging operator to establish a unified inversion model with relational constraints. To solve this model, a deep learning network named SSI-net is constructed by integrating the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMMs) with deep unfolding techniques. Besides, for scenarios with limited or no training samples, a gradient descent-based optimization algorithm is also proposed. Simulations and experiments with measured data demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed framework.
author: Jun'ao Li, Zhongyu Li, Jing Yang, Qing Yang, Junjie Wu

title: A Physically Interpretable Neural Optimization Solver for InSAR Phase Unwrapping
content: Phase unwrapping (PU) is a fundamental yet ill-posed problem in interferometric synthetic aperture radar (InSAR), where the measured wrapped phase must be converted into a continuous absolute phase field. Conventional path-following and optimization-based PU methods often suffer from degraded performance in areas with abrupt interferometric fringe change. Moreover, most weighted norm formulations are susceptible to numerical imbalance, while deep learning (DL)-based models often suffer from limited interpretability and physical consistency. To overcome these limitations, this article proposes a physically interpretable neural optimization solver, termed adaptive neural iterative solver (ANISolver), for InSAR PU. First, a geometric tessellation regularization (GTR) is introduced to represent the topographic surface as locally planar patches, encouraging differentiability of the phase surface. Second, an adaptive reweighted fidelity (ARF) mechanism is developed to stabilize numerical imbalance in traditional weighted norm optimization and to dynamically adjust fidelity weights based on the phase gradient field. Third, an artificial intelligence (AI)-based interpretable optimization solver is designed, where an untrained convolutional neural network (UCNN) acts as a physics-guided optimizer integrated with a fast approximate conjugate gradient (FA-CG) algorithm, enabling efficient computation without the need for training data. Both theoretical analysis and experimental validation demonstrate the efficacy and efficiency of the proposed method.
author: Sijia Chen, Hanwen Yu, Taoli Yang, Yan Yan

title: Language-Inspired Hierarchical Mamba Network With Superpixel-Structured Training for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Existing Mamba-based hyperspectral image classification (HSIC) methods typically decouple spectral and spatial modeling into two independent and parallel branches and fuse their features afterward. Such physical separation risks information loss or feature conflicts when the fusion strategy is suboptimal, ultimately degrading the classification accuracy. To address this issue, we propose a language-inspired hierarchical Mamba network with superpixel-structured training (L-HMambaS) that unifies the spectral and spatial dimensions from the outset. Specifically, inspired by the structure of natural language, we regard the spectrum of a pixel as a “word” and the spatial arrangement of multiple pixels as a “sentence” and devise a nested Mamba architecture. In the L-HMambaS model, an inner spectral Mamba [inner word-level mamba (ISM)] block captures cross-band dependencies among pixels, while an outer spatial Mamba (OSM) block explores local contextual relations among spatial patches. Furthermore, to suppress redundancy induced by overlapping patches in conventional sampling strategies, we introduce a superpixel-driven sampling strategy that guarantees high intrapatch homogeneity and minimal spatial overlap. Extensive experiments on five publicly available hyperspectral datasets, namely, Pavia University, WHU-Hi-LongKou, WHU-Hi-HanChuan, WHU-Hi-HongHu, and Houston 2013, demonstrate that L-HMambaS achieves overall accuracies of 97.01%, 98.17%, 92.91%, 93.37%, and 96.79% with only 30 labeled samples per class, consistently surpassing state-of-the-art competitors. The codes will be available from the website: https://github.com/rachelitem/L-HMambaS
author: Hao Liu, Xiang Xu, Qiong Yao, Antonio Plaza

title: Implicit Continuous Full-Spectrum Response Network for Visible-to-Infrared Image Translation
content: The acquisition of infrared remote sensing imagery is often constrained by high sensor costs and complex atmospheric conditions, leading to a scarcity of infrared data. While data-driven visible-to-infrared image translation has demonstrated potential, existing methods typically neglect to merge sensor imaging mechanisms. To bridge this gap, a joint data-mechanism-driven visible-to-infrared translation framework, named implicit continuous full-spectrum response network (ImconfuseNet), is proposed, which consists of an implicit full-spectrum reconstruction module (IFSM) and two spectral response imaging modules (SRIMs). The sensor imaging process intended for modeling comprises two fundamental steps: the continuous radiation energy incident on the sensor, and the sensor responds to the energy via a specific spectral response function (SRF). In order to model the radiation energy, the IFSM learns a spatial–spectral-wise continuous representation, which projects a visible image into a continuous full-spectrum image spanning visible and infrared wavelengths. Afterward, two SRIMs simulate the sensor-specific response process to generate the infrared (and visible) images. Moreover, we construct a similar imaging mechanism-driven infrared-to-visible reverse generator and adopt a cycle-consistent training scheme to enforce domain consistency. The major contribution of ImconfuseNet is that we realize visible-to-infrared image translation by incorporating the spectral response imaging process and integrating it into a data-driven neural network. Experimental results indicate that the proposed network outperforms several state-of-the-art methods. Our code will be available at https://github.com/qffang/ImconfuseNet
author: Qiaoying Qu, Bin Pan, Ruiying Li, Zhenwei Shi

title: An Information-Theoretic Latent Disentanglement Framework for Multimodal Hyperspectral Image Compression
content: Existing single-modality hyperspectral image compression methods often lack explicit structural constraints in the latent space. Most methods rely on pixel-level rate–distortion objectives. Spatial–spectral consistency with physical meaning becomes difficult to preserve under high compression ratios. Multimodal representation learning often depends on empirical modeling. Theoretical guidance remains limited. To address these issues, an information-theoretic constrained framework for multimodal hyperspectral image compression is proposed. Auxiliary modality information provides structural guidance for the latent space. Latent representations are explicitly disentangled into modality-specific and shared components. Mutual information-based regularization is introduced to suppress statistical dependence. The regularization is integrated into the rate–distortion optimization framework. In addition, a best neighbor matching mechanism is introduced. Cross-modal spatial features are aligned. Structural consistency in the latent space is further enhanced. Experiments on mixed multimodal hyperspectral datasets demonstrate superior performance over existing single-modality compression methods. Improvements are observed in both rate–distortion performance and spectral consistency.
author: Jiahui Liu, Lili Zhang, Xinbo Li

title: BAPA-Net: Cross-Domain Few-Shot Object Detection in Remote Sensing via Background-Aligned Prototype Aggregation
content: While large-scale supervised learning has catalyzed remarkable strides in remote sensing object detection (RSOD), its real-world efficacy is frequently stifled by the formidable costs of expert annotation and the inherent scarcity of high-quality samples. Cross-domain few-shot object detection (CD-FSOD) has emerged as a strategic paradigm to alleviate this dependency by facilitating knowledge transfer from label-rich natural domains to data-starved remote sensing environments. However, the pronounced geospatial domain shifts and intricate background interference often lead to suboptimal generalization in existing frameworks. To address these impediments, we introduce the Background-Aligned Prototype Aggregation Network (BAPA-Net), a consolidated framework meticulously crafted to enhance the invariance of cross-domain representations. Specifically, BAPA-Net integrates three pivotal mechanisms: 1) Cross-Domain Background Fusion Alignment (CBFA), which decouples and synchronizes source–target background distributions through an innovative sampling–clustering–aggregation pipeline, effectively mitigating low-level feature interference; 2) Soft-Mask-Guided Prototype Augmentation (SMPA), which employs dynamic probabilistic Gaussian masks to construct a continuous spatial attention mechanism, thereby filtering peripheral noise and refining prototype representativeness; and 3) Prototype Retrieval and Knowledge Aggregation (PRKA), which leverages a cross-domain Cache bank and attention-weighted retrieval to aggregate multisource information adaptively, ensuring persistent exploitation of limited labeled samples. Extensive evaluations across diverse benchmarks, including high-altitude, underwater, and deep-space scenarios, demonstrate that BAPA-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methodologies. Specifically, our framework achieves substantial performance gains, improving the mAP by 5.0% to 10.8% relative to the baseline across 1, 5, and 10-shot protocols. Our results validate the framework’s robustness in bridging the semantic gap between natural and remote sensing domains. The code is open source: https://github.com/JackLeeAI/BAPA-Net
author: Chao Li, Qi Zhang, Xiao Liang, Yuhang Xiao, Shenzhi Li, Peng Wang

title: Parallel Global Convolution-Enhanced Spatial Chunk Transformer Network for Few-Shot Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: Hyperspectral image (HSI) classification under few-shot scenarios remains a challenging task due to the high dimensionality of HSI data and the scarcity of labeled samples. Traditional convolutional neural networks (CNNs) struggle to capture long-range spectral–spatial dependencies due to limited local receptive fields, while pure Transformers suffer from O((HW)2) high computational complexity and weak local feature extraction capability, making them poorly adaptive to few-shot HSI classification. To address these issues, we propose a parallel global convolution-enhanced spatial chunk Transformer network (PGC-SCTN), which realizes progressive spectral–spatial feature optimization via three core modules. Specifically, a deep convolutional embedding (DCE) module mines high-dimensional local fine-grained spectral–spatial features of HSI. A parallel global feature enhancement extraction (PGFE) module then adopts horizontal–vertical separated convolution to efficiently capture long-range spectral–spatial dependencies, compensating for the global modeling deficiency of CNNs. Furthermore, a spatial chunk Transformer (SCT) module decomposes 2-D self-attention into 1-D chunk computation along the height and width directions, reducing the computational complexity of global dependency modeling from O((HW)2) to O(HW*(H + W)) and balancing global semantic refinement with computational efficiency. Experiments on four public hyperspectral datasets show that the proposed network outperforms traditional methods and state-of-the-art (SOTA) algorithms in classification performance, demonstrating superior robustness and generalization.
author: Jun Pang, Lina Yang, Xinzhang Wu, Wanxing Zhu, Lianlian Zhao

title: FS-CrossMamba: Dual-Branch Frequency-Guided Spatial and Spectral-Attention-Driven Spectral Cross-Mamba Network for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: The hyperspectral image classification (HSIC) is intrinsically constrained by insignificant interclass discrepancies and high-spectral redundancy. Although Mamba-based methods have appeared recently, current techniques still struggle to properly address the minor differences between classes and substantial spectral redundancy present in HSIC. To overcome these issues, we introduce the dual-branch frequency-guided spatial and spectral-attention-driven spectral Cross-Mamba network (FS-CrossMamba). Unlike conventional methods that estimate state transition parameters solely from the input sequence, our method explicitly injects auxiliary priors into the SSM state transition parameters through two specialized branches: the frequency-domain-guided spatial Cross-Mamba (FSCM) branch, exploiting amplitude and phase priors to guide spatial selective scanning and preserve structural coherence, and the spectral-attention-driven spectral Cross-Mamba (SASCM) branch, utilizing global spectral attention to reweight spectral responses and strengthen long-range dependency modeling. Furthermore, we design a triple-branch convolutional adaptive spectral–spatial coupling module (TCASSCM) to model rich multiscale spectral–spatial relationships as the initial embedding. Comprehensive experiments on five benchmark datasets demonstrate that FS-CrossMamba reliably achieves state-of-the-art results in comparison with top-performing convolutional neural network (CNN)-, Transformer-, and Mamba-based approaches.
author: Tianxiang Zhang, Gongcai Zhang, Wenxin Jia, Yuxin Zeng, Mengting Hou, Jiangyun Li

title: TALS-CD: Causal Continuity-Based Task-Aware Mamba for Remote Sensing Binary Change Detection
content: Scan strategies are critical for Mamba-based visual tasks, yet predefined handcrafted scan paths often fail when applied to task-specific data. In change detection (CD), irregular discontinuities between changed and unchanged regions disrupt spatial continuity, causing existing scan methods to increase effective dependency lengths and reduce the causal effectiveness of Mamba-based change feature representations. To address these challenges, a task-aware learnable scan (TALS) strategy is proposed for Mamba-based CD (TALS-CD). TALS learns causal correlations between local windows and establishes an interwindow priority sequence for bitemporal difference feature representation learning. With TALS, Mamba first models the global dependencies of different features and then progressively refines them in a top-down manner, leveraging the local properties of CNNs. In addition, a mixture of difference expert (MoDE) is introduced to utilize complementary difference representations, dynamically selecting optimal routing paths to enhance difference feature expression. Experimental results on three public datasets demonstrate that TALS-CD achieves superior CD performance with a small number of parameters and computational cost. The code is available at: https://github.com/fangcunxinghe/TALS-CD
author: Leiquan Wang, Lifa Xu, Chunbo Luo, Chunlei Wu

title: Adapting Self-Supervised Vision Foundation Models for Cross-Domain Surface Water Mapping
content: Accurate and scalable mapping of surface water is crucial for understanding environmental dynamics, ensuring sustainable water resource management, and facilitating informed decision-making. However, the extraction of surface water at the global scale remains challenging due to the scarcity of labeled samples, as well as substantial spatial heterogeneity across regions. Traditional deep learning methods frequently fail to generalize effectively under these conditions. With the advent of vision foundation models (VFMs), such as DINOv3, visual representation learning has demonstrated enhanced generalization and transferability across domains. However, their potential for downstream remote sensing applications, particularly in surface water mapping (SWM), remains underexplored. In this study, we propose SWM-DINOv3, a framework designed to efficiently adapt the self-supervised DINOv3 model for large-scale, cross-domain SWM. The framework employs a pretrained DINOv3 as the encoder and a LinkNet architecture as the decoder. The pretrained DINOv3 parameters are kept frozen, while parameter-efficient fine-tuning is performed using a general-purpose low-rank adaptation (LoRA) module and two task-specific adapters. Among these, a pyramid pooling adapter (PPA) is integrated to address the large-scale variability of water bodies by extracting multiscale contextual features, while an atrous depthwise convolution adapter (ADCA) introduces lightweight local inductive biases, improving boundary delineation for water bodies with low texture and fragmented shorelines. Extensive experiments conducted on two global-scale datasets—OpenEarthMap and GLH-water—demonstrate that SWM-DINOv3 effectively leverages the generalization capabilities of VFMs, achieving superior accuracy and robustness in cross-domain SWM compared to existing VFM-based approaches. Notably, SWM-DINOv3 outperforms segment anything model (SAM)-based methods by over 10% in  $F1$ -score. This work establishes a pivotal and scalable solution for global water resource monitoring and management.
author: Xiaoyan Lu, Qihao Weng

title: Cross-Modality Self-Distillation for Visible-Infrared Person Re-Identification
content: Visible-infrared person re-identification (VI-ReID) faces large challenges due to the cross-modality gap and intraclass variations. Existing methods mainly focus on mitigating the cross-modality discrepancy by optimizing the embedding space of high-level features. However, two critical issues are often overlooked: low-level features suffer from larger modality gaps and redundant channels exhibit limited identity discrimination. These limitations hinder the overall discriminative performance of the model. To address the above issues, we propose a novel cross-modality self-distillation (CMSD) network, which consists of two key components: cross-layer semantic distillation (CSD) and intralayer alignment distillation (IAD). These two modules are mutually beneficial to jointly explore cross-layer modality discrepancy and intralayer channel discrepancy. Specifically, CSD distills modality-shared semantics from high-level features to guide low-level features in learning more reliable modality-invariant representations. Furthermore, IAD decomposes feature maps into salient and redundant channels while transferring identity-related information from salient to redundant channels, thereby enhancing overall discrimination of modality-invariant features. Our extensive experiments on the SYSU-MM01, RegDB, and LLCM datasets demonstrate that the components of our proposed CMSD mutually enhance each other, leading to superior performance compared to state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods.
author: Zihao Hu, Yongguo Ling, Zhiming Luo, Wenhao Shao, Shaozi Li, Zhun Zhong

title: KGDNet: Knowledge-Guided Decoupled Modeling of Heterogeneous Change Mechanisms for Remote Sensing Change Detection
content: High-resolution remote sensing change detection (RSCD) methods typically rely on single-modal image features, making them highly susceptible to pseudo-changes induced by factors like illumination, shadows, and seasonal variations. Moreover, most approaches adopt a unified binary change paradigm, overlooking the heterogeneous mechanisms of change, such as appearance and disappearance. To address these limitations, this article presents KGDNet, a knowledge-guided tri-branch network designed for decoupled modeling of heterogeneous change mechanisms in change detection (CD). KGDNet leverages annotation-free monocular depth maps as geometric priors and, through the guided alignment and fusion module (GAFM), upgrades 2-D features to 3-D geometry-aware representations. This enhances the discrimination of genuine land-cover changes while effectively suppressing pseudo-changes at the source. The network explicitly decouples three core mechanisms—appearance, difference, and disappearance—using specialized parallel branches to capture distinct change patterns. Additionally, the difference branch dynamically corrects and refines prior knowledge, mitigating contamination from low-quality depth cues. To efficiently process high-resolution imagery, we further propose a multihead latent spatiotemporal attention (MH-LSTA) module that compresses and remaps features in a latent space, substantially reducing computational and memory costs while enhancing the modeling of global spatiotemporal relations. This yields a favorable tradeoff between accuracy and efficiency. Comprehensive experiments on public benchmarks demonstrate that KGDNet outperforms representative state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods in key metrics, delivering superior detection accuracy and robustness. This study proposes a knowledge-guided decoupled modeling paradigm that integrates prior knowledge with heterogeneous change mechanisms to enhance the interpretability of change representations. The code and data will be released upon publication at https://github.com/yangzy1217/KGDNet
author: Zongyu Yang, Haitao Li, Haiyan Gu, Yi Yang, Shaopeng Ding, Haozhu Kong

title: Corrections to “On the Sea Surface Effective Reflection Coefficient and Mean Square Slope at Microwave Backscattering”
content: In [1], while the relationships among $mss_{xx}$ , $mss_{yy}$ , $mss_{xy}$ , the ERC, and wind speed are discussed in the main text, practical application has shown that the formulas and coefficients in Appendix B can be further refined with more accurate coefficients. To enhance usability without altering the original results or conclusions, the formulas and coefficients in Appendix B are hereby updated. Specifically, the original Appendix B is replaced by the version provided in this article. Equation (11) in [1] is a three-parameter formula, whereas the formulas for Figs. 15 and 17 utilize only two parameters, and that for Fig. 16 employs only one parameter in this article.
author: Xiuzhong Li, Vladimir Karaev, Mariya Panfilova, Dmitrii Kovaldov, Baochang Liu, Shuyan Lang, Yijun He

title: WANet: A Wavelet-Attention Network for Accurate Ditch Extraction in Remote Sensing Imagery
content: Ditches are important for the irrigation, drainage, and water resource regulation of agriculture. However, their narrow, linear, and fragmented morphologies pose significant challenges for remote sensing extraction. Existing water body datasets mostly focus on large-scale targets such as lakes and rivers, while systematic research on small linear water bodies such as ditches is still relatively limited. To improve research on small linear water bodies, we constructed a remote sensing dataset covering the North China Plain, which contains manually and finely annotated samples for ditches and a small number of rivers. We further propose an innovative detail and semantic enhancement network. This network is built on a CNN–Transformer hybrid backbone, incorporating a wavelet transform feature decomposition module and a fine-grained attention mechanism to enhance multiscale representation and boundary precision in ditch extraction. These modules jointly improve the model's ability to capture detailed and small-scale structures in complex remote sensing scenes. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model achieves an IoU of 81.52% and a Precision of 86.83%, outperforming other state-of-the-art methods. The constructed dataset and the wavelet attention framework will be helpful for ditch extraction in remote sensing and agricultural applications.
author: Yanfeng Zhang, Zhiwu Gao, Ying Dai, Yu Wu, Lei Zhang, Xingxing Han, Zhenxuan Liu

title: IGFUL: An Integration Gap-Filling, Unmixing, and Learning Approach for Estimating Continuous High-Resolution Landsat Normalized Difference Vegetation Index Time Series
content: The normalized difference vegetation index (NDVI) is the most popular index used in remote sensing for monitoring vegetation dynamics. While numerous moderate-resolution NDVI products are available, there is an increasing demand for fine-resolution, temporally continuous NDVI time series, particularly for monitoring vegetation dynamics in areas with high surface heterogeneity. A major challenge in producing such datasets is the irregular Landsat data acquisition, often hampered by extensive cloud contamination. This article introduces an integration gap-filling, unmixing, and learning (IGFUL) approach for creating continuous 30-m, 8-day NDVI time series by integrating Landsat surface reflectance with 250-m GLASS NDVI data. The workflow comprises four stages: 1) constructing the first random forest (RF) model using cloud-free Landsat surface reflectance as input and GLASS NDVI as output to obtain physically consistent Landsat clear-sky NDVI; 2) for cloudy pixels on a target day, identifying similar pixels based on seasonal similarity curves and utilizing their spatiotemporal correlation information for prefilling missing Landsat NDVI values; 3) identifying reference images using structural similarity index, calculating temporal and spatial predictions through unmixing and thin plate spline methods respectively, and developing the second RF model using temporal prediction, spatial prediction, GLASS NDVI on the target day, and Landsat NDVI and GLASS NDVI on the base day as inputs, with Landsat NDVI on the target day as output; and 4) weighting multiple initial predictions from the second RF model to generate the final Landsat NDVI reconstruction results. IGFUL was evaluated under varying cloud conditions and outperformed three benchmark methods in both accuracy and efficiency. It achieved high accuracy at the Coleambally Irrigation Area site [root-mean-square error (RMSE) = 0.013 and correlation coefficient (CC) = 0.983] and remained robust at the cloud-heavy Qionglai site (RMSE = 0.033 and CC = 0.882). IGFUL’s ability to reduce reconstruction errors while preserving phenological and spatiotemporal consistency underscores its potential for generating high-resolution and temporally continuous NDVI time series in challenging remote sensing environments.
author: Guodong Zhang, Jiangchuan Hu, Gaofei Yin, Rui Chen, Changjing Wang, Meilian Wang, Yi Zhang, Zongyan Li, Yimin Ni, Gang Sun, Yufang Zhang, Anxin Ding, Xinyan Liu, Aleixandre Verger

title: Correcting Wave-Induced Reflectance Anomalies in Pan-Sharpened Very High-Resolution Satellite Data
content: The increasing spatial resolution of satellite sensors – now reaching sub-meter scale – enables advanced applications in aquatic remote sensing, including highly detailed bathymetry or habitat mapping. However, this fine resolution also increases pixel-level susceptibility to wave glint and whitecap contamination. These wave-induced reflectance anomalies (WIA) can severely degrade image quality and reduce the accuracy of derived products. Although strategic acquisition planning can help to minimize WIA, users often have limited control over satellite tasking. As a result, WIA contamination is difficult to prevent in practice. In this study, we present a novel WIA correction method for pan-sharpened Pléiades-1 imagery that detects and reconstructs affected pixel values. WIA features are identified with an ensemble of similarity-weighted support vector machines, which exploit spatial curvature and near-infrared reflectance. Detected anomalies are then corrected with a spatially adaptive gap-filling technique. Our method operates on top-of-atmosphere reflectances, allowing users flexibility in further data preprocessing steps like atmospheric correction. Our approach demonstrates very high accuracy in WIA detection and shows comparable variance reductions in optically deep water compared to the popular Hedley glint correction and the water anomaly filter. In optically shallow environments, our method outperforms these benchmarks by effectively removing WIA while preserving benthic spatial detail. Requiring minimal parameter tuning, the approach is well-suited for integration into automated preprocessing pipelines. It provides a practical tool for improving data quality and enhancing the reliability of downstream applications. Our approach is also easily adaptable to other very high-resolution sensors with similar spatial resolutions.
author: Eike M. Schütt, Daniyal Kazempour, Peer Kröger, Natascha Oppelt

title: A Spatial-Spectral Component Representation Network for Fine-Grained Mineral Classification in Hyperspectral Imagery
content: Mineral mapping is a significant focus in remote sensing research, which aims to fully utilize spectral data for identifying and describing the composition of surface minerals. To gain a deeper understanding of mineral distribution within the study area, fine-grained hyperspectral image classification (HSIC) has emerged as an important research challenge. In mineral scenes where mineral co-occurrence and severe spectral mixing are prevalent, variations in mineral abundances largely govern discriminability, making abundance information essential for improving the reliability of mineral identification. Motivated by these considerations, we develop a spatial-spectral component representation network for HSIC, called SSCRNet, which introduces an autoencoder-structured unmixing network to achieve complementary integration between abundance features and deep features extracted by the network. The SSCRNet enhances deep classification networks by introducing convolutional layers with different dilation rates, which enables the simultaneous capture of fine-grained mineral texture details and large-scale geological structural patterns in mineral areas. This design effectively improves the classification performance for complex surface types. Experimental results demonstrate that SSCRNet outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches in classification performance and robustness for mineral mapping scenarios.
author: Zhaoyu Wang, Mingming Xu, Shanwei Liu, Hongxia Zheng, Muhammad Yasir, Qinlong Tong

title: Rof-Mamba: Rotary-and-Fourier-Enhanced Mamba Network for Remote Sensing Image Super-Resolution
content: Remote sensing image super-resolution technology holds significant importance in large-scale scene analysis, but existing methods struggle to effectively balance high-frequency detail recovery and long-range dependency modeling. To address this issue, this article proposes an efficient super-resolution network with collaborative frequency and state-space modeling, called the Rotary-and-Fourier-enhanced Mamba network (Rof-Mamba). This method, while maintaining the advantage of linear complexity, embeds a frequency perception unit into the state-space modeling framework, enhancing high-frequency texture representation by strengthening key frequency responses. It also integrates a two-dimensional vision state space module modeling to achieve efficient global dependency propagation and improve structural consistency. In addition, a rotary-gated linear attention module is introduced, combining rotary position encoding with locally enhanced position encoding within the linear attention mechanism to enhance spatial position awareness and local structure alignment, thereby realizing collaborative optimization of spatial, frequency, and positional multidomain information. Compared with traditional convolutional or pure attention-based methods, the proposed model can more stably restore fine-grained structures and boundary details in complex remote sensing scenes. The ×4 super-resolution experimental results on the AID, DOTA, and DIOR datasets show that Rof-Mamba achieves average peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR)/structural similarity index measure(SSIM) of 31.983 dB and 0.8350 with a lightweight configuration of only 4.81M parameters and 83.85G FLOPs. Compared with the MambaIR of the same parameter scale, the average PSNR and SSIM increased by 0.113 dB and 0.0016, respectively.
author: Jiawei Yang, Hongliang Ren

title: SAR-LiteDETR: A Lightweight Detector for Small Ships in SAR Images
content: Small shipdetection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery is challenged by speckle noise, strong scale variation, and complex sea clutter, especially under practical efficiency requirements for SAR-based maritime surveillance. We propose SAR-LiteDETR, a lightweight transformer-based detector tailored for maritime SAR scenes. SAR-LiteDETR includes three lightweight plug-and-play modules: PConv-Blocks; an attention-guided feature pyramid network, and a channelwise multibranch fusion block. These three modules improve backbone efficiency and small-target sensitivity, stabilize multiscale feature alignment under clutter, and adaptively fuse cross-level features to suppress sea clutter and alleviate feature dilution, respectively, while keeping computational cost low. Experiments on the SSDD, HRSID, and LS-SSDD datasets show that SAR-LiteDETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art and baseline detectors. In particular, it achieves $AP_{S}$ and precision of 69.6%/97.0% on SSDD and 54.2%/93.5% on HRSID, respectively, while requiring only 42.1 GFLOPs. The results indicate that SAR-LiteDETR provides a favorable balance between detection accuracy and efficiency for near real-time SAR-based maritime surveillance.
author: Zhen Yang, Haowei Chen, Zhijian Yin, Xin Zhou, Lijun Deng

title: A High-Resolution Inversion Framework for Cropland Soil Organic Carbon Estimation Using an Ensemble Machine-Learning Approach
content: Cropland soil organic carbon (SOC) is a critical soil component governing soil fertility, agricultural productivity, food security, climate change mitigation, and ecosystem health via carbon biogeochemical cycling. Conventional soil sampling features low spatial density and infrequent updates, making it incapable of high-resolution, large-scale SOC monitoring. Meanwhile, individual machine-learning models show weak generalization across heterogeneous agroecological conditions and limited accuracy in fine-scale SOC inversion. To tackle these constraints, this article developed a stacked ensemble metamodel framework for Tianjin’s croplands to achieve high-precision 10-m resolution surface SOC inversion and mapping. Thirty-six biophysical and spectral covariates including normalized difference vegetation index, enhanced vegetation index, and green normalized difference vegetation index (GNDVI) were derived from Sentinel-2 imagery as predictors. Four base learners, namely random forest, support vector regression (SVR), eXtreme gradient boosting, and elastic net regression,, were trained and verified, with ridge regression as the metalearner. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) analysis was used to quantify feature contributions for model interpretability. The ensemble model obtained an R2 of 0.7816, 0.1069 higher than the best-performing base model SVR (R2 = 0.6747), and reduced root mean square error by 18.0% to 1.6274 g/kg, greatly enhancing prediction accuracy and stability. SHAP results identified SVR predictions, GNDVI, and B4/B11 as the main drivers of SOC spatial variability. The 10-m SOC map revealed obvious spatial heterogeneity: higher SOC in northern Tianjin (Jizhou, Wuqing, and Baodi) and lower levels in Binhai New Area and Jinghai District, consistent with regional soil texture and land management intensity. This framework offsets defects of single models, providing a robust foundation for assessing carbon sequestration potential, formulating agricultural carbon management strategies and supporting precision agriculture.
author: Zhaoxu Zhang, Yuchen Qiu, Sijia Du, Dan Wan, Yuanheng Sun, Haitao Wang

title: Central Dynamic Transformer for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: In hyperspectral image (HSI) classification, patch-based methods have evolved from treating all pixels equally to acknowledging the importance of central pixels whose labels determine classification outcomes. However, existing central attention approaches suffer from two limitations: they rely on static linear transformations that apply fixed weights uniformly across inputs, and they focus solely on the central pixel while neglecting other same-object pixels, leading to incomplete representations. To address these limitations, we propose the Central Dynamic Transformer (CDTrans) through two innovations. First, the Central Dynamic Linear (CDLinear) module replaces static projections with spatially adaptive transformations, modulating key and value representations based on global context and central pixel characteristics. Second, Central Dynamic Attention (CDAttn) module employs a two-stage mechanism: identifying the $K$ most semantically similar pixels through cosine similarity in the CDLinear-transformed feature space, then performing attention where selected pixels attend to all pixels. This enables discriminative object-level feature extraction. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate that CDTrans achieves state-of-the-art performance, with ablation studies validating the significant contributions of both CDLinear and CDAttn.
author: Zhibin Zhang, Huan Liu, Zhiyang Zheng, Wenyu Qu, Wei Li

title: Onboard Real-Time Hyperspectral Subpixel Mapping With Pushbroom Neural Network
content: Satellite hyperspectral images provide fine spectral information but often suffer from coarse spatial resolution. This may result in mixed pixels containing multiple land cover classes, degrading the performance of conventional classification methods. Subpixel mapping (SPM) seeks to assign class labels to multiple subpixels within each coarse pixel, producing a super-resolved classification map. Existing approaches rely on priors that are either handcrafted or obtained via large deep learning architectures for offline processing requiring substantial memory and computational resources. In this work, we focus on the processing setting directly onboard the satellite, where such priors are not available and there are strict constraints on computational complexity. In particular, we propose a line-based deep SPM network tailored for real-time deployment on satellites with a processing strategy that aligns with the pushbroom acquisition of hyperspectral sensors, named deep pushbroom subpixel mapping. The model predicts a super-resolved classification map exploiting spatial–spectral context from neighboring lines, along with long-range information from previous lines through efficient state-space models. The method achieves fast inference and has low memory requirements compatible with resource-constrained satellite platforms. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves state-of-the-art subpixel hyperspectral mapping accuracy, while supporting real-time inference at the line acquisition rate, as tested on a low-power accelerator.
author: Matteo Impieri, Davide Piccinini, Diego Valsesia, Enrico Magli

title: Chem-YOLO: A Multiattention YOLO for Remote Sensing Detection of Chemical Industrial Park Facilities
content: Chemical industrial parks (CIPs) are characterized by dense facilities, complex structures, and high safety risks, necessitating efficient and accurate intelligent supervision. However, high-resolution remote sensing image target detection for CIPs still faces numerous challenges, including difficulties in identifying small targets, confusion of dense targets, severe geometric distortions, and complex background interference. To address these issues, this article proposes a high-precision and robust chemical facility detection model, Chem-YOLO, based on the YOLOv12 architecture. This model incorporates three new modules: the vanilla object structure feature extraction module enhances the perception ability for small targets by emphasizing high-frequency structural features; the global context-aware module aggregates global context information to effectively suppress background noise; and the geometric texture module aims to enhance the feature representation of targets with strong geometric constraints. Extensive experiments conducted on a self-built high-resolution dataset show that Chem-YOLO achieves an mAP50 of 0.7945 and an F1-Score of 0.7771, outperforming mainstream models, such as YOLOv12 and RT-DETR. Furthermore, in practical applications of continuous four-year temporal monitoring, the model demonstrates strong temporal robustness, not only accurately quantifying the spatiotemporal evolution of park facilities but also effectively identifying areas of unauthorized expansion. Experimental results demonstrate that Chem-YOLO offers an effective technical solution for intelligent supervision of CIPs, with significant potential for engineering applications.
author: Tianhao Wang, Shixin Wang, Futao Wang, Zhenqing Wang, Litao Wang, Xingguang Gu, Yanchao Wang, Luoyao Hao, Zhaowei Wang, Haojie Tao, Jinfeng Zhu, Wenliang Liu

title: D-PolTransGAN: Scattering Guided Hybrid Transformer-GAN for SAR-Optical Image Translation Using Dual Polarization SAR
content: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) provides reliable imaging in all weather and lighting conditions, but its speckled appearance makes visual interpretation difficult and limits its integration with optical-based analyses. To address this challenge, we propose a new SAR-to-optical image translation framework tailored for flood assessment. The proposed model processes SAR VV and VH polarizations through separate feature extraction streams, enabling each stem to learn its unique scattering characteristics. These features are then fused using cross-attention to capture complementary information from both polarizations. The architecture combines convolutional networks for local detail extraction with transformer modules to model global context, all within a generative adversarial learning framework. We train and evaluate the model using the DEEPFLOOD, SEN1FLOODS11, and SEN12MS datasets, which provides well-aligned Sentinel-1 (VV, VH) and Sentinel-2 image pairs. Experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms existing approaches in terms of PSNR, SSIM, and perceptual quality, while preserving fine spatial details critical for flood analysis. Qualitative results on real flood events further demonstrate that the generated optical-like images recover visual structures and contextual information that are difficult to interpret in raw SAR imagery.
author: Jagrati Talreja, Tewodros Syum Gebre, Leila Hashemi-Beni

title: Measuring Greenland Ice Sheet Melt Based on GNSS-R and Random Forest Using FengYun-3E GNOS-R
content: As surface melting of the Greenland Ice Sheet (GrIS) is an important driver of sea-level rise against the backdrop of accelerating global climate system warming, high-precision monitoring of its state is crucial for understanding the polar-atmosphere coupling mechanism. The melting detection is mainly based on a microwave radiometer with a spatial resolution of only tens of kilometers, while Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R) can have a higher spatial resolution of kilometers. GNSS-R ice sheet melting detection mainly uses the reflectivity threshold method, which has low accuracy and lacks discussion of physical mechanisms. This study utilizes Delay-Doppler Map data from the GNOS-R sensor aboard China’s Feng Yun-3E polar-orbiting satellite to construct an ice-sheet melting detection model based on the random forest algorithm with multiple parameters innovatively, achieving spatiotemporal dynamic inversion of the surface freeze-thaw processes over GrIS. Through comparative and combinatorial experiments with four designed feature subsets (waveform-related parameters, power-related parameters, geometric condition constraints, and spatial location constraints), it is found that increasing feature dimensions and complementarity of physical meanings significantly enhance model performance, ensuring stable and accurate freeze-thaw detection under different scattering mechanisms. The optimal model demonstrates excellent performance in multimetric evaluations, with many evaluation metrics exceeding 96%. The spatial resolution is greatly improved while maintaining similar accuracy to the microwave radiometer. Through combined experiments on different parameter types, this study further reveals the regulatory effect of the incident angle on the scattering mechanism and characteristic response during the surface melting process of the ice sheet. Its physical essence lies in the differences in dielectric loss and dielectric uniformity of the GNSS signal between the frozen and melt state of snow at different incident angles. In summary, this study provides a solution combining physical interpretability and computational efficiency for complex scene classification of multisource remote sensing data, holding significant scientific implications for improving input parameters of climate system models and enhancing the prediction accuracy of sea-level rise.
author: Liangyu Chen, Jiachun An, Zemin Wang, Baojun Zhang, Jingxin Gao

title: SAFE: A SAR Feature Extractor Based on Self-Supervised Learning and Masked Siamese ViTs
content: Due to its all-weather and day-and-night capabilities, synthetic aperture radar imagery is essential for various applications such as disaster management, Earth monitoring, change detection, and target recognition. However, the scarcity of labeled synthetic aperture radar (SAR) data limits the performance of most deep learning algorithms. To address this issue, we propose a self-supervised learning framework based on masked Siamese vision transformers to learn transferable SAR representations, coined SAFE. Our method leverages contrastive learning principles to train a model on unlabeled SAR data, extracting robust features. Rather than introducing a new learning paradigm, the contribution lies in the adaptation of self-supervised learning to SAR data through modality-aware design choices. SAFE is applicable across multiple SAR acquisition modes and resolutions. We introduce tailored data augmentation techniques specific to SAR imagery, such as subaperture decomposition and despeckling, designed to preserve SAR-consistent invariances. Comprehensive evaluations on various downstream tasks, including few-shot classification, segmentation, visualization, and pattern detection, demonstrate the effectiveness and versatility of the proposed approach. In particular, cross-dataset evaluations show that the learned representations transfer across different sensors and acquisition conditions. Our network yields strong performance in few-shot classification and segmentation tasks, even without being trained on the sensors used for evaluation, highlighting its potential for transferable representation learning in SAR applications.
author: Max Muzeau, Joana Frontera-Pons, Chengfang Ren, Jean-Philippe Ovarlez
